summary: English Baroque composer (c. 1659–1695), student of John Blow, and organist at Westminster Abbey. Best known for the opera Dido and Aeneas (especially the 'Lament'), and for semi-operas such as King Arthur, The Fairy-Queen, and Dioclesian. His incidental music to Abdelazer provided the theme Benjamin Britten used in The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Henry Purcell (c. 1659–1695) was an English Baroque composer and student of John Blow, whom he succeeded as principal organist at Westminster Abbey. He modeled the final chorus of Dido and Aeneas on the final chorus of Blow's Venus and Adonis. His works are catalogued using Z numbers (compiled by musicologist Franklin Zimmerman). Michael Nyman quoted Purcell in works including 'Chasing Sheep is Best Left to Shepherds' and 'Memorial.' He is commonly attributed as the author of the Irish march Lilliburlero. His brother Michael and son Edward were also composers. His posthumous collection of songs was called Orpheus Britannicus.

works.name: Dido and Aeneas – Dido's Lament ('When I am laid in earth')
works.description: The climactic final aria of Dido and Aeneas, in G minor. Preceded by the secco recitative 'Thy hand, Belinda.' The opening melody descends with the repeating phrase 'Remember me!' in a dotted rhythm, followed by 'but ah! Forget my fate.' The basso ostinato is a chromatic descending fourth (G to D) — a 'lament bass' or chromatic fourth, also called passus duriusculus — with a two-measure cadential return; each repetition is 5 bars (unusual for a ground bass). The handmaid Belinda witnesses Dido's death. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with a chromatic descending bass from G to D (chromatic fourth / lament bass); unique 5-bar ground bass repetition
examples: The basso ostinato for this aria consists of a chromatic stepwise descent over the interval of a fourth and a two-measure cadence returning to the tonic G.
Name this aria, which opens with a chromatic descending bass pattern from G down to D. This aria features a unique instance of a ground bass since each repetition has a length of 5 bars.

clues.clue: Preceded by the secco recitative 'Thy hand, Belinda, darkness shades me / On thy bosom let me rest'
examples: The recitativo secco that precedes this aria opens with the lines 'Thy hand, Belinda, darkness shades me / On thy bosom let me rest.'
The secco recitative 'Thy hand, Belinda' introduces this aria.

clues.clue: Dido sings 'Remember me, but ah! Forget my fate'; also 'May my wrongs create / No trouble in thy breast'
examples: This composer is probably best known for an opera whose best aria repeats the line 'Remember me, but ah, forget my fate.'
Name this operatic character who sings a dotted rhythm on three repetitions of the phrase 'Remember me!' before singing a descending melody on 'but ah! Forget my fate.'

clues.clue: Handmaid Belinda (Dido's sister) witnesses the lament; she is the one beseeched
examples: This character, Dido's sister, and Second Woman are handmaidens of Dido. She is the one beseeched in 'Dido's Lament'.
In one opera by this composer, one of the title characters sings a dying aria set over a ground bass... with Belinda as witness

works.name: Dido and Aeneas
works.description: Purcell's only true opera, with a libretto by Nahum Tate. Depicts the love story of Dido, Queen of Carthage, and the Trojan hero Aeneas (from Virgil's Aeneid). An evil sorceress tricks Aeneas into abandoning Dido. The opera includes a chorus of witches and a chorus of sailors. Purcell modeled the final chorus ('With drooping wings') on the final chorus of Blow's Venus and Adonis. Heavily influenced by Blow's Venus and Adonis and by Cavalli's Didone. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Libretto by Nahum Tate; evil sorceress tricks Aeneas into abandoning Dido
examples: Nahum Tate wrote the libretto for this opera in which an evil sorceress tricks one of the title lovers into abandoning the other.
Dido and Aeneas, an opera by this English Baroque composer

clues.clue: Includes chorus of witches and chorus of sailors
examples: A chorus of witches and a chorus of sailors are characters in an opera by this composer

clues.clue: Final chorus 'With drooping wings' modeled on the final chorus of Blow's Venus and Adonis
examples: This composer succeeded his mentor as principal organist at the Westminster Abbey, and modeled one opera's final chorus, With drooping wings, on the final chorus of Venus and Adonis by that mentor, John Blow.

clues.clue: Influenced by Blow's Venus and Adonis and Cavalli's Didone
examples: Besides its basis in Virgil's Aeneid and influence from Cavalli's Didone, Dido and Aeneas was heavily influenced by this Baroque composer's Venus and Adonis.

works.name: King Arthur (semi-opera)
works.description: A semi-opera with libretto by John Dryden. Arthur rescues the blind princess Emmeline from the Saxon King Oswald. Includes the 'Frost Scene' (Cold Genius aria 'What Power art thou,' sung by Cupid) and the aria 'Fairest Isle.' The 'Cold Song' from this opera (with lyrics about a spirit frozen in ice, sung by a countertenor) was used by Klaus Nomi and covered by Olga Neuwirth. A fanfare on 'Fairest Isle' was used as the BBC Radio 3 theme for a 1995 celebration of British music. Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'Frost Scene': Cupid sings 'What Power art thou' (Cold Genius aria) — countertenor set over a ground bass describing snakes from Fury Alecto's head, in three-bar C minor ground bass
examples: Cupid sings the aria 'What Power art thou' in the 'Frost Scene' of this man's opera King Arthur.
He used a three-bar ground bass in C minor for an aria often sung by a countertenor, whose lyrics describe snakes dropping from the head of the Fury Alecto.

clues.clue: Libretto by John Dryden; Arthur rescues blind princess Emmeline from Saxon King Oswald
examples: With a libretto by John Dryden, King Arthur's music was written by this composer.
this 17th century semi-opera in which the title character rescues the blind princess Emmeline from the Saxon King Oswald.

clues.clue: 'Fairest Isle' aria; a fanfare on it was used as the BBC Radio 3 1995 British music celebration theme
examples: A fanfare based on 'Fairest Isle' from this opera was used as a theme for BBC Radio 3's 1995 celebration of British music.

works.name: The Fairy-Queen (semi-opera)
works.description: A semi-opera based on Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream, with a 'Drunken Poet' character added. Includes the Chinese Woman's aria 'Hark! Now the Echoing Air' preceded by the 'Monkey's Dance,' and Juno singing 'Thrice Happy Lovers' and 'The Plaint.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Based on A Midsummer Night's Dream, with the character of the Drunken Poet added
examples: He also wrote the 'Monkey's Dance,' which is followed by the Chinese Woman's aria 'Hark! Now the Echoing Air,' in a work in which Juno sings 'Thrice Happy Lovers' and 'The Plaint,' and the character of The Drunken Poet is added to an adaptation of A Midsummer Night's Dream.
This English Baroque composer of Ode to St. Cecilia... He adapted William Shakespeare's play A Midsummer Night's Dream into his opera The Fairy-Queen.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Purcell's other semi-operas include Dioclesian (subtitled 'The Prophetess,' named for a Roman emperor) and the unfinished The Indian Queen (set in Mexico; its opening sinfonia was reused from the ode 'Come, Ye Sons of Art'). 'Music for a While' is a countertenor song from the incidental music for a play on the Oedipus story, later published in Orpheus Britannicus. He also wrote a chaconne in G minor (arranged by Britten).

clues.clue: 'Music for a While' — countertenor song in incidental music for an Oedipus-based play; published posthumously in Orpheus Britannicus
examples: Today, countertenors often perform this composer's Music for a While.
He included the ground-bass song 'Music for a while' in his incidental music for a play on the Oedipus story

clues.clue: Dioclesian (semi-opera named for a Roman emperor; subtitled 'The Prophetess')

comprehensive_summary:
Henry Purcell (c. 1659–1695) was an English Baroque composer and student of John Blow, whom he succeeded as principal organist at Westminster Abbey. His works are catalogued by Z numbers, compiled by musicologist Franklin Zimmerman. His brother Michael and son Edward were also composers.

Purcell's only true opera is Dido and Aeneas, with a libretto by Nahum Tate. It depicts the love affair between Dido, Queen of Carthage, and the Trojan hero Aeneas, who is tricked by an evil sorceress into abandoning her. The opera was heavily influenced by Blow's Venus and Adonis (Purcell modeled his final chorus 'With drooping wings' on Blow's) and by Cavalli's Didone. Its climax is 'Dido's Lament' ('When I am laid in earth') — the singer pleads 'Remember me, but ah! Forget my fate' — preceded by the recitative 'Thy hand, Belinda.' The lament is built on a chromatic descending ground bass (G down to D, a chromatic fourth / passus duriusculus), with each repetition lasting an unusual 5 bars. Dido's handmaid Belinda witnesses her death.

Purcell's semi-operas include King Arthur (libretto by John Dryden, in which Arthur rescues the blind princess Emmeline from Saxon King Oswald), The Fairy-Queen (adapted from A Midsummer Night's Dream, adding the Drunken Poet character), Dioclesian ('The Prophetess'), and the unfinished The Indian Queen. King Arthur contains the 'Frost Scene' in which Cupid sings 'What Power art thou' over a three-bar ground bass in C minor, with lyrics about snakes from the head of Fury Alecto — the aria is often performed by a countertenor. The opera's 'Fairest Isle' aria was the basis for a BBC Radio 3 1995 celebration of British music. 'Music for a While,' a countertenor song from Purcell's incidental music for an Oedipus-based play, was later published in his posthumous collection Orpheus Britannicus.

Purcell's incidental music for Aphra Behn's Abdelazer provided the rondeau theme Benjamin Britten used in The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra (Variations and Fugue on a Theme of Purcell). He composed music for Queen Mary II's funeral — a C minor March for four flatt (slide) trumpets and a Canzona — and the setting of 'Thou Knowest Lord.' His birthday ode 'Come, Ye Sons of Art' (honoring Queen Mary II) features the countertenor duet 'Sound the Trumpet'; its opening sinfonia was recycled in The Indian Queen. His Ode to St. Cecilia ('Hail! Bright Cecilia') includes the movement 'Wondrous Machine!' in praise of the organ; his Te Deum was the first English Te Deum with orchestral accompaniment. William Croft used Purcell's setting of 'Thou Knowest Lord' as a homage. Michael Nyman quoted Purcell in 'Chasing Sheep is Best Left to Shepherds' and 'Memorial'; Purcell is also credited with the Irish march Lilliburlero.
